> I am still unsure why we keep copying these AST documents instead of linking to them.

Well we also do link, but that does not give any real benefits. We do copy those files because then we do have the changes_from<old version>to<new version>.rst files that includes a diff for us in the documentation. This way we directly see any changes within the AST grammar. That is extremely helpful for a systematic and structered maintanance approach. 

> I think it is a bit early for this document. It may still change during the release process.
Yes it will change during the release process, but that does not make it wrong to copy it as early as possible. The currently copied is actually the version from 3.11 as a base, so thzat we could later also quickly see in our editors if there has been any changes on an update. 
This Document should be adapted while checking an upcoming release candidate of a new Python version.

Well, they're different in that a head node has additional things.
Often the head node requires different configuration than worker nodes. (This is the case in our sample configs.)
For large workloads we often recommend hacking the head node with num-cpus=0 to avoid scheduling Ray workloads there and make it more-or-less into a control node.

If the head node is a special kind of worker node, I guess "worker" is redundant -- we just have nodes, one of which is a head. Eliminating the terminology "worker node" altogether would also reduce the conflation between "worker nodes" and "worker processes", but I think this ship has sailed.

While we will see there is an error if the customers send us the logs due to the warning (the warning should definitely include the error itself here!), the fallback might not be clear to the user as the warning is easily missed. This can lead to increased support loads over a immediate failure with (ideally clear) error messages.

In my opinion, for such a case, we should print the complete error returned by the docker daemon (the whole "this directory is not shared with the VM" thing), and abort. As a user, I would like it better for the software to fail hard at the first time with a clear error message, than to get unexpected behavior later (no persistence working for example), after the warning is already burried in the logs.

We can do so, however, the NAT64 is only meanful on Border Routers.

- If we allow the user to specify a NAT64 prefix, then the border routing might be work incorrectly since the advertised prefix might be different than the one in effect.
  - If the user sets a prefix, and BORDER_ROUTING is enabled, that means we cannot be a NAT64 capable BR unless we are configured as "auto prefix discovery"
- The next hop (or previous hop) of the message will never in thread network (since thread is IPv6 only) the NAT64 will only work on a border router.
  - That means the router already has IPv4 access, and it does not need a NAT64 translator.

Seems the only possible case is to be a standalone NAT64 translator, but I don't think it is our priority now.

I think it is possible to decouple the NAT64 translator from BR, but I don't think we need to allow users to configure the NAT64 prefix before further dfscussions around these APIs.

try:

I've seen us talk about "Session going out of scope". I don't think this is right. `__exit()__` only gets called when the object was created as a context manager. Customers may and do sometimes use these Session objects by invoking the constructor "normally".

Consider:

```
class ContextManager():
    def __init__(self):
        print('init method called')

    def __enter__(self):
        print('enter method called')
        return self

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, exc_traceback):
        print('exit method called')

    def __del__(self):
        print('del method called')


print("Test as context manager")

with ContextManager() as manager:
    print('with statement block')

print("\nTest as regular variable")

if True:
    c = ContextManager();

print("The end")
```

outputs:

```
Test as context manager
init method called
enter method called
with statement block
exit method called

Test as regular variable
init method called
The end
del method called
del method called
```

I think we may want to ensure that the appropriate thing happens on `__del()__` if it `__exit()__` never got called.
We also need the documentation to cover both cases.

').join(output)` that follows. Having a blank line causes the previous unterminated line and a new blank line to be joined using `

`, with the previous line, giving an additional unwanted `
`.

I implemented the One-Shot Aggregation (OSA) module from [VoVNet](https://arxiv.org/abs/1904.09730) architecture:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1671644/92328848-8da32600-f09e-11ea-8a9d-7134d53cb446.png)

Then I added the the effective Sequeeze and Excitation module (eSE) from [VoVNet V2](https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.06667) (right-most diagram without the identity mapping, aka residual):

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1671644/92328397-6bf46f80-f09b-11ea-8333-6c5bf5c41b1f.png)

You can see the printed architecture here, as well as the tensor sizes at each layer for an input of 224x224 after a `pyramid_down<6>`. The gist of it is:
- downsample by 4 with 2 `relu<bn_con<con<M, 3, 3, 2, 2, SUBNET>>>`
- OSA Module:
  - concatenate the input + the output of 3 sequential `relu<bn_con<con<M, 3, 3, 1, 1, SUBNET>>>`
  - eSE module: scale the previous output with the result of `sig<con<N, 1, 1, 1, 1,avg_pool_everything, SUBNET>>`
- max_pool, OSA Module, max_pool, OSA module
- detection head
```
layer<0>	loss_mmod	 (detector_windows:(horse:80x74,55x80;person:74x80,50x80), loss per FA:1, loss per miss:1, truth match IOU thresh:0.5, use_bounding_box_regression:1, bbr_lambda:100, overlaps_nms:(0.5,1), overlaps_ignore:(0.5,0.95))
layer<1>	output size=(num:1, k:20,   nr:54,  nc:16)	con	 (num_filters=20, nr=5, nc=5, stride_y=1, stride_x=1, padding_y=2, padding_x=2) learning_rate_mult=1 weight_decay_mult=1 bias_learning_rate_mult=1 bias_weight_decay_mult=0
layer<2>	output size=(num:1, k:384,  nr:54,  nc:16)	scale1
layer<3>	output size=(num:1, k:384,  nr:1,   nc:1) 	sig

However, after tinkering with it,   I found that it is useful for seeing which files have a lot of branching (and thus should be given test attention, but it didn't provide enough information to go right to the branch locations. Idrk what the right answer is with feature gating. I think for sure we want to land this feature, as it's a really helpful capability. My concern with feature gating is that you might turn it off without realizing the need. For example, Toolchain used to be `>=3.8` always. Recently, we made a portion of our codebase `>=3.7`. While it's not the end of the world if we use `>=3.8` on that too, we actually do want the partitioning imo because it will mean MyPy can check that we're not using Py38 syntax on accident. I don't think we would have realized we should turn on this partitioning when making the change; it's subtle.

Feature gating also makes this code more complex, although that's not something we should optimize too much for imo.

On the other hand, 1.5 seconds when Pants is already super slow with MyPy isn't great.

1. The overview describes the problem and relates to the target audience (relevant to you if you run multi-container Docker applications), and then gives a glimpse to the solution. Kinda similar to an abstract in a CS paper.

It's not that I wouldn't prefer a home grown, tastefully themed solution, but there's some problems:

- The Dialog box from @jupyterlab/apputils just isn't that flexible. It hard to significantly customize or use in complex scenarios.

- The apputils Dialog also isn't React based. The way it implements events (like what happens when you click a button) aren't naturally compatible with standard React patterns.

Because of the above, I once wasted an entire evening trying to implement a custom form using the Dialog from jlab core. I wanted to open the dialog box using one React component that was nested within a tree, then update the top-level component in the tree based on the user's input. It was not working out. On the other hand, once I decided to switch to the Dialog from material-ui, I had it solved within about 15 min.

I definitely see your point Frederic about homogeneity, and about the value of keeping things simple and consistent. It is true that material-ui brings along its own complex config system (with a rather foreign set of conventions). However, I don't think it makes sense for us to favor any particular approach based on a primary argument of homogeneity, or "because it's provided by jlab core". Instead, we should ask ourselves which component will allow us to build the most kick-ass and easily maintained `jupyterlab-git` that we can manage. The Dialog component from material-ui is simply much better than the Dialog from jlab core, even more so since we're primarily working within React here.

So based on my own experience (and I suppose I am still a bit salty about the apputils Dialog wasting my time), I think that Athan absolutely made the right call here to use material-ui. My only caveat is the same as Frederic's: the theming overall needs to mesh with that of the rest of jlab (though it certainly doesn't have to perfectly match).

On the horizon of a month or two from now, a React-based dialog box is definitely on my list of things to add to @jupyterlab/ui-components. Once that exists, maybe it will be appropriate to revisit this discussion.

This is to accommodate differences in the format of the module portion of member tokens. Ideally, all of our providers would use the following format:
```
pkg:namespace:member
```
Where `namespace` is a slash-separated path. The k8s provider uses this format today, e.g.:
```
kubernetes:meta/v1:ObjectMeta
```

Unfortunately, the TF providers do something a bit different, and format the type token like so:
```
pkg:namespace/member:member
```

Using a regex to extract the namespace allows us to accommodate this difference without aliasing every resource defined by a TF provider.

And then the question is, how do you know if a **`python_test`** cares about the extensions and expects implementations to be installed? For instance, some of the tests call an rest API to get the list of runners - there is no explicit import or magic string in the API layer that says it needs the runners to be present.

For me, I discover which tests need things through trial and error - try running the test, see if it breaks, and then add the namespaces to fix the errors raised (it will loudly complain if it can't find a requested extension/plugin/driver/... _aside: there are so many names for approximately the same thing. ugh_)

Today, StackStorm's old Makefile just installs all of these extensions/plugins/drivers (eg the runners) before running any of the tests. But, loading all of the runners is a very heavy weight dependency, so I wanted a way to narrow the scope of what needs all of that. This way I'm opting in only specific tests to making those extensions available.

> aux is the original series (shown in faded color) when smoothing is enabled

Concretely, I think dropping L111 changes our cases from this:
If line segment is [number && singlePoint && aux] skip
If line segment is [number && non-aux] draw
If line segment is [NaN && aux] skip
If line segment is [NaN && non-aux] draw

Into this:
If line segment is [number] draw
If line segment is [NaN && aux] skip
If line segment is [NaN && non-aux] draw

Is there something we gain by keeping L111, besides rendering fewer points in the single point scenario, that I might be missing?

I did some digging; the first v1 version of this test came in PR #812, which was a bug-fix for issue #811, which @lgray opened, having discovered the bug through Coffea. That's where the references to electrons come from.

In the original issue, only `leptons.pt` is passed to `argsort`: numbers, not the particle objects. The original issue was about `leptons` being a union array, calling `.pt` on it and getting a "union of numbers and numbers", calling `argsort` on that and getting a "union of integers and integers", and then not being able to use a union array as a slice. That would be a case of a missing `simplify_uniontype`, which I think you've addressed in another test here.

Yes, your fix of src/awkward/_v2/contents/unionarray.py is putting a `simplify_uniontype` in `_getitem_field` (and `_getitem_fields`), which would fix issue #811 by making `leptons.pt` return a non-union array of numbers.

So I think it's fine to delete this test; it's being tested elsewhere.

if that is the case, we can probably parametrize this test case to vary the configuration options, but we still assert the same result.

For example:

```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize("dd_trace_debug", ["true", "false", None])
@pytext.mark.parametrize("dd_trace_log_file", [True, False])
def test_unrelated_logger_loaded_last(run_python_code_in_subprocess, tmpdir, dd_trace_debug, dd_trace_log_file):
    env = os.environ.copy()
    if dd_trace_debug is not None:
        env["DD_TRACE_DEBUG"] = dd_trace_debug
    if dd_trace_log_file:
        env["DD_TRACE_LOG_FILE"] = tmpdir + "/testlog.log"

    code = """
import ddtrace
import logging
custom_logger = logging.getLogger('custom')
custom_logger.setLevel(logging.WARN)
assert custom_logger.parent.name == 'root'
assert custom_logger.level == logging.WARN
"""

    out, err, status, pid = run_python_code_in_subprocess(code, env=env)
    assert status == 0, err
    assert err != b""
    assert out == b""
```

With parametrize this way you will end up testing this test scenario with:

- DD_TRACE_DEBUG set to true and DD_TRACE_LOG_FILE set
- DD_TRACE_DEBUG set to true and DD_TRACE_LOG_FILE not set
- DD_TRACE_DEBUG set to false and DD_TRACE_LOG_FILE set
- DD_TRACE_DEBUG set to false and DD_TRACE_LOG_FILE not set
- DD_TRACE_DEBUG not set and DD_TRACE_LOG_FILE set
- DD_TRACE_DEBUG not set and DD_TRACE_LOG_FILE not set

```pycon
>>> def f(x): return x in (1, 2, 3)

>>> def g(x): return x in [1, 2, 3]
... 
>>> def h(x): return x in {1, 2, 3}
... 
>>> import dis
>>> dis.dis(f)
  1           0 LOAD_FAST                0 (x)
              2 LOAD_CONST               1 ((1, 2, 3))
              4 CONTAINS_OP              0
              6 RETURN_VALUE

  1           0 LOAD_FAST                0 (x)
              2 LOAD_CONST               1 ((1, 2, 3))
              4 CONTAINS_OP              0
              6 RETURN_VALUE

  1           0 LOAD_FAST                0 (x)

```

This one is a tricky one, technically the `wrap` and `unwrap` methods of `NtlmContext()` should be used instead but unfortunately `unwrap` on `NtlmContext` has a different signature than the one of session_security. While this works I don't really want to rely on `_` prefixed attributes because they aren't really guaranteed to stay stable over different releases.

What I recommend is to add a session_security property to this class like so:

```
@property
def session_security(self):
    warnings.warn("Deprecated property session_security, the wrap and unwrap methods should be accessed using the host context ntlm.contexts['hostname'].wrap/unwrap instead.", DeprecationWarning)
    return next(iter(self.contexts))._session_security
```

This also requires us to store the `NtlmContext` as a property in requests-ntlm. This is done in a similar way with requests-kerberos where the context is stored in a dict that's indexed with the hostname of the request. You can see that [requests-kerberos](https://github.com/requests/requests-kerberos/blob/master/requests_kerberos/kerberos_.py) uses urlparse `from requests.compat import urlparse` to get the hostname from the request and that is passed into the retry method. From there the context that is initialized is stored in `self.contexts[hostname]`.

Doing this means that other libraries that rely on the wrapping and unwrapping functions provided by session_security aren't broken in the next release, are warned about it being deprecated here and are offered the alternative.

The httplib2.CookieJar is not a file storage.  It's a cookie jar in memory with functions to load cookies from/or save to file. Just like `get()/set()/clear()`,  the `get_header()` is not special, it means getting the cookies in the format of HTTP header. Similarly,  `extract_header` means setting the cookies from HTTP header. 

Maybe it will be much clear if separating the FileCookieJar from the CookieJar.  For example:
```python
class CookieJarBase(CookieJar):
       <null operations>
 
NullCookieJar = CookieJarBase

class CookieJar(CookieJarBase):
        1. Virtual interfaces to persist the cookies: load()/save() 
        2. Implement all other operations(also includes `get_header`/`extract_header`) on self._cookies

class FileCookieJar(CookieJar):
        Re-implement save()/load() interface with file storage

class SqliteCookieJar(CookieJar):  <-- your example
        Re-implement save()/load() interface with sqlite database
```

`aiocontextvars` is a third-party package that offers a stand-in replacement for Python 3.7's `contextvars` in the stdlib. It is generally preferred over `threading.local` because it actually works with asyncio, *however*, in applications that use gunicorn/gevent/eventlet, `threading.local` works better than either contextvars or aiocontextvars. The reason for *that* is because gevent monkeypatches the threading module to behave correctly, but is not doing the same thing for contextvars or aiocontextvars.

This is why this logic became that contrived. We test this by having multiple virtualenvs with gevent/eventlet installed/not installed and initialized/not initialized.

I have a whole philosophy around this ;)

I think longer, descriptive names are important when you have a large namespace to hold in your head and the names help you remember what that object is. An alternative strategy is to keep namespaces small so you don't have to keep track of lots of things.

In this file there's only one "thing" being imported from the outside, so I'd say its pretty easy to remember what's going on. Pretty much any name would work, but I think shorter names keep the code easier to read. (I'm even a little upset I have to use two whole characters here, but the underscore helps hide the symbol from the end user.)

The example I always think of is a squaring function like

```python
def square(thing_to_be_squared):
    result_of_squaring = thing_to_be_squared*thing_to_be_squared

    return result_of_squaring
```

when something like

```python
def square(x):
    return x*x
```

is probably **more** clear, even though `x` isn't as descriptive of a name. The namespace of the function is so small that the name doesn't really matter, as everything can be figured out from context.

That said, I'm still completely open if you have other suggestions!

Agreed that the private `_parse_psm3` function is better.

I'll nitpick that using `hasattr(obj, 'method')` is an example of duck typing since it's asking "does `filename` `readline()` like a duck?", but I agree that trying to open the file would be more pythonic -- I'm just not sure that combining it with `with open...` is a good idea.  The try/except is wrapping the `_parse_psm3` call as well as the `open()` call when really we only want to catch errors from `open()` and I'm not sure it's necessarily possible to only catch errors from the `open` call.  Catching errors from both would cause the function to try to interpret a filename as a file-like object instead of raising a useful error.  For instance calling `open` on a `StringIO` raises a TypeError so we would have to trap `TypeError`, causing any TypeError raised in `_parse_psm3` to make the code try going down the other path and raise `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'readline'` as the primary error -- not great.  

I'm not opposed to using try/except for duck typing, but I'm not sure there's a way to safely combine it with a `with open(..) as fbuf:` call.  I don't like storing file handles outside of `with` and keeping track of whether or not to close the object, but I can't think of a better way to separate errors from `open()` and `_parse_psm3`:

```
def read_psm3(fbuf):
    """..."""
    try:
        # using rb causes error later on with fbuf.readline().split(',')
        fbuf = open(filename, 'r')
        close = True
    except TypeError:
        fbuf = filename
        close = False
    finally:
    
    try:
        content = _parse_psm3(fbuf)
    finally:
        if close:
            fbuf.close()
    return content
```

I know using try/except is generally looked on more favorably than attribute testing (EAFP vs LBYL), but to me it seems better to just ask the object "can I `readline` you?" than try to figure out what exceptions `open` might raise.  I'm not even sure that the above code is correct -- if `open` can raise TypeError in other situations besides when we give it a StringIO, we'd want to propagate that to the user.

In case you're interested, I got the `hasattr` pattern from [pandas's CSV writing code](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/master/pandas/io/formats/csvs.py#L177), though I think the additional complexity of that situation warrants the more flexible solution. 

Also RE: the file mode -- using 'rb' would require some other changes since you have to split a bytes object with a bytes object, not a str.  The tests pass on windows for me, so do we need to worry about it?

Here you're comparing the `origin_hash`, as it's written in the JSON file, to the `returned_hash`, which has been put through `hexdigest()`, so it's no comparing apples to apples. 

For instance, if you put `origin_hash = 'anOriginMd5'` through `hash_type(json_data).hexdigest()` you'll get `'39f5b5db96c26ca61e13b280c8b98b6e'`, so you're comparing `'anOriginMd5 == '39f5b5db96c26ca61e13b280c8b98b6e'`, which will return false.

So, you'll need to compare `hash_type(origin_json_data).hexdigest() == hash_type(otherfile_json_data).hexdigest()`

> What would `&1` mean? Is it referring to reference to an i32 `1`?

Yep! (where the `i32` part is implied from type-checking)

> If so, is it stable and refers to the same value to L81?

Nope! It’s like:

```rust
m.insert(Tag("accuracy".to_string()), 1);  // line 81

let tmp = 1;
assert_eq!(m.get("accuracy"), Some(&tmp));  // line 84
// `tmp` is implicitly dropped here
```

We can’t get a reference to the `1` from line 81; not only is it out of
scope, it probably never had a stack location at all (would be passed in
a register for this signature). But that’s okay, because equality on
[references] is defined in terms of equality on the underlying values:

> In addition, the comparison operators transparently defer to the
> referent's implementation, allowing references to be compared the same
> as owned values.

You can get reference equality semantics by casting to pointers. This
is safe: it’s only *dereferencing* pointers that’s unsafe. But I’ve
never had to use this, even once. Value equality is basically always

@stuhood that's how things work in spirit, but you cannot actually partially specify a tag, it must be a complete tag. That tag can contain elements that make it looser though like `any` and `none` in certain slots. For the python ecosystem, the flecibility is provided by platform sets. The match within a set must be exact, but the set itself contains both exact platform matches and looser ones too. Unfortunately the logic for generating the sets is not codified in any PEP I know and the defacto standard only exists in https://github.com/pypa/packaging/blob/master/packaging/tags.py#L805.

The problem I have with the current setup is that all of this code is available to your application when installing confluent-kafka even without the dependencies. This means it won't  be caught until runtime which is sloppy. Instead we should move these to their own top-level packages which can be referenced as extras when installing confluent-kafka. 

So confluent-kafka-avro, confluent-kafka-json, confluent-kafka-schema-registry would all be their own projects which could be optionally installed with extras from the main confluent-kafka package. The serializer packages would list confluent-kafka-schema-registry as a dependency insuring its installed along with the serializer. 

With this setup installation would still look like this `pip install confluent-kafka[avro, json]`. Except none of these packages would be available for import until explicitly installed.

So, the unclosed file warning stems from setting `pipe = PIPE` in the non-debug case. It disappears if we set it to `pipe=subprocess.DEVNULL` instead. 

But then we get a different `ResourceWarning`: `subprocess is still running`

The warning was added in 3.6, here: https://bugs.python.org/issue26741
Pretty much everyone saying keeping a zombie process is bad practice, but here we go.

The followup issue mentions some potential solutions to hide the warning, but `detach` didn't work: https://bugs.python.org/issue27068

So I had a look at the CPython source, turns out if we set the returncode manually (since we don't care about it anyway), the warning will not be raised: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/85e5b1f5b806289744ef9a5a13dabfb23044f713/Lib/subprocess.py#L1127 (from main, just to make it a permalink)

If there are filters, but the index is not a range index, the `else` here is the right thing: just concatenate the indices. They may have repeated values, etc, which is fine.

A range index is the special case: we want the Modin dataframe to have a _single_ range, not a range that keeps restarting. i.e. if the partitions have index 0-9, 0-19, 0-29, we want our Modin dataframe to have 0-59. If I understand correctly, that is why this case exists:

https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/5b297a146e83d99a29fcfcaa3dc7dc20b60d02e4/modin/core/io/column_stores/parquet_dispatcher.py#L535-L538

by momentarily making a single Pandas dataframe, it can get what the full range would be, and not the ranges used within each partition.

So, I could have made this work with filters by passing the filters through to `to_pandas_dataframe` there. But that would actually require performing the filters, which is not a cheap operation like reading the index, and would have to be repeated again when the workers actually load in the data. So I figured having them do so, then using the materialized indices, was better. Let me know if I have missed something here.

> when can we have a model in results.info that's not in results.models

All the time, since this PR also expands `info` so it includes history on every model at ([source](https://github.com/dask/dask-ml/pull/370/files#diff-0a54ded74c8013caf0588e9a5c879e99R260)). I've added that to https://github.com/dask/dask-ml/pull/370#issue-216493783.

> Under what conditions can we end up in a situation where the highest-scoring model isn't ~~returned to us here~~ in `results.models`?

There are two dimensions to the adaptive search:

1. how many `partial_fit` calls happened before a model was scored
2. how high the score is

For adaptive searches (which includes stop on plateau), there's no guarantee that the highest scoring model be scored with the maximum number of `partial_fit` calls.

For passive searches (e.g., `RandomizedSearchCV`) there's only one dimension: how high the final score is.

Here is a suggested rewording of the documentation:

GatherElements takes two inputs `data` and `indices` of the same rank r >= 1 and an optional attribute axis that identifies an axis of `data` (by default, the outer-most axis, that is axis 0). It is an indexing operation that produces its output by indexing into the input `data` tensor at index positions determined by elements of the `indices` tensor. Its output shape is the same as the shape of `indices` and consists of one value (gathered from the `data`) for each element in `indices`.

For instance, in the 3-D case (r = 3), the output produced is determined by the following equations:
```
  out[i][j][k] = input[index[i][j][k]][j][k]  # if dim == 0
  out[i][j][k] = input[i][index[i][j][k]][k]  # if dim == 1
  out[i][j][k] = input[i][j][index[i][j][k]]  # if dim == 2
```
This operator is the inverse of ScatterElements.

Example 1:
```
  data = [
      [1, 2],
      [3, 4],
  ]
  indices = [
      [0, 0],
      [1, 0],
  ]
  axis = 1
  output = [
      [
        [1, 1],
        [4, 3],
      ],
  ]
```

Example 2:
```
  data = [
      [1, 2, 3],
      [4, 5, 6],
      [7, 8, 9],
  ]
  indices = [
      [1, 2, 0],
      [2, 0, 0],
  ]
  axis = 0
  output = [
      [
        [4, 8, 3],
        [7, 2, 3],
      ],
  ]
  ```

The resolution is sequential. That means, the resolution is not commutative.
`left.join(right, on='A', alter_name={'C':'F', 'F': 'G'})
out put will be <A,B,C,D,F,G>. This is useful to swap names iff collision is detected.

The way how it works is simple, the solver first loads all names from left sframe.

A,B,C,D
A,B,C,D <- when adding 'C' into table name list, a collision is detected, it just replace 'C' to 'F', regardless the fact 'F' will be added later. As long as for all names visited, there's no collision, the resolution attempt will proceed.
A,B,C,D,F <- 'F', trivial.
```

Looking at the code more closely when trying to make the change, it seems there are some differences in the 4 maps.
- for `state` and `location`, they currently track all the actors in the system, and it's natural to merge them;
- for `rpc client`, it only tracks the actors that we submit tasks to, I think after the subscribe can support individual actor, then we can have `state` and `location` to only have the actors that we submit tasks to, at that point it would be natural to merge this with them.
- for `pending tasks`, I'm feeling it's probably better to keep it separate, as we can have pending tasks before an actor is created, if we merge this together with the rest maps, we will have an entry with `state, location, rpc client` uninitialized, thus I would prefer to keep this separate.

I've checked the  `libkvm` sources and `/usr/src/bin/ps/ps.c` on all three BSD variants; they all use `kvm_err()` if there's a failing `kvm_*()` call.

I've written a tiny standalone test program to check for all return values (on all three platforms).
Fortunately, they all behave the same.

- `kvm_open*` isn't likely to fail with `EACCESS`, as it doesn't open `/dev/[k]mem` as in the old days.
It will only fail if the library cannot allocate internal memory, or cannot open the kernel boot file. `errno` will be set appropriate.
The question is which exception should be raised - `RuntimeError` or `OSError`?

- `kvm_getprocs` isn't likely to fail _itself_.
If the search for the PID fails, `cnt` is set to `0`. If the function itself returns `NULL`, it means that it was not able to allocate memory for the result, or the call parameters were invalid due to a programming error (`&cnt` pointing to invalid storage).
We can raise `RuntimeError` or `OSError` if the call itself failed.
If the call reports `cnt == 0`, this maps to `ESRCH` ("no such process").

I understand your concern mapping system errors to different Python errors;
now from what I've tested, `errno` is set appropriate after all failing `libkvm` calls.

There is just _one_ race which comes to my mind: When calling `kvm_getprocs()`, it may return success.
But before our call to `kvm_getenvv()`, we might have been preempted by the kernel, and the process in question might have terminated.
In this case, our later call to `kvm_getenvv()` will return `EINVAL`, and we probably should remap this to `ESRCH`?

The reason being that `pickle._Pickler.dump.__name__` is simply `dump`, whereas to retrieve this function, we need to get the qualified name of this function, namely `_Pickler.dump`

If we use `__qualname__` instead of `__name__`, we cannot use `getattr` though, because `getattr` does not accept dotted path. Instead, we need to use `pickle._getattribute`.

Each field is comprised of a HEADER + BODY.  The HEADER consists of a FIELD_TYPE + FIELD_LENGTH. The size of the HEADER is always 5 bytes (1 for the type and 4 (32 bits) for the length). So that is why we always have to read the header this way.

I think this suggests otherwise:

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/751c856953060d68ae7be2c62306d11935e32345/trinity/utils/chains.py#L123-L163

In the case of  `__init__(args, trinity_config)` the result is that the `__init__` method must parse and normalize the values from the `argparse.Namespace` object which I would argue is a bad coupling,  binding it to the CLI

In the case of `__init__(trinity_config, arg_1, arg_2, ...)` and `Config.from_parser_args(args, trinity_config)` we still have *coupling* but it is looser and keeps the `cli_args -> init_args` conversion from bleeding into the actual configuration objects implementation.

It looks like you're trying to address the fact that `object_detector::train` depends on `nn_spec_` being non-`NULL` (as well as `training_model_`), after the call to `init_train`. So we just need to set these data members manually in `init_train_impl`. Unfortunately, to this end we should probably add protected setters to the base class, e.g. `set_nn_spec` and `set_training_model`... for now. (In the longer term, I think the class can be refactored a bit, so that e.g. `init_train` returns all the things that `init_train` actually initializes.)

Then the body of `init_train_impl` looks something more like:
```
model.set_training_model(std::unique_ptr<model_backend>(new mock_model_backend));
model.set_nn_spec(std::unique_ptr<nn_spec>(new nn_spec));
```

Note that all `object_detector::train` does with `training_model_` is call `export_weights`, and a default `mock_model_backend` already returns an empty `float_array_map`, which is fine.

Then `object_detector::train` will call `nn_spec_->update_params` with an empty `float_array_map`, which should also be fine.

Because I want to exactly match the format of `end_time` with `start_time` when we parse them using `parser.isoparse()`. See the following example:
1. `start_time` is copied directly from attribute `container.attrs['State']['StartedAt']`. When we parse the `start_time` using `parser.isoparse`, there is the `tzinfo` in the return value.
```
>>> start = "2019-11-26T01:15:54.7762612Z"
>>> parser.isoparse(start)
datetime.datetime(2019, 11, 26, 1, 15, 54, 776261, tzinfo=tzutc())
```
2. However, if we use `datetime.datetime.utcnow()` without specifing `tzinfo`, there is no `tzinfo` attribute return in the datetime object.
```
>>> parser.isoparse(str(datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat()))
datetime.datetime(2019, 11, 26, 5, 38, 38, 745842)
```
3. When we explicitly specified `tzinfo` in `datetime` object, we also get the `tzinfo` in the return result.
```
>>> end=str(datetime.datetime.now().replace(tzinfo=tz.tzutc()))
>>> parser.isoparse(end)
datetime.datetime(2019, 11, 26, 5, 36, 41, 91737, tzinfo=tzutc())
```
Therefore, I decided to chose `str(datetime.datetime.now().replace(tzinfo=tz.tzutc()))` instead of `datetime.datetime.utcnow()`. Let me know if we want to swith over.

A few nits for this exception handling:
1. Small exception message nits.
2. We should (1) raise a `RuntimeError` here instead of a generic exception.
3. We should use `from e` exception chaining to make the relationship of these two exceptions clear.
```
{{caught error traceback}}
The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:
{{outer failed TFRecord read error traceback}}
```
Just doing `raise RuntimeError(error_message)` without the chaining would result in
```
{{caught error traceback}}
During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:
{{outer failed TFRecord read error traceback}}
```
which makes it look like we were trying to handle `e` but failed, with a different error being raised.
```suggestion
        except Exception as e:
            error_message = (
                f"Failed to read TFRecord file {path}. Please ensure that the TFRecord file "
                f"has correct format. Already read {row_count} rows."
            )
            if data_length is not None:
                error_message += f" Byte size of current record data is {data_length}."
            raise RuntimeError(error_message) from e
```

I think you should keep the original style when `CHECK_EQUAL(my_data, 1)` yields an error message showing that it was `my_data` that failed. It gives the user some extra context information to distinguish different checks from each other even if no message is supplied. In general you can have an output like this:

...Got \<first argument>=\<the erroneous value> expected \<prefix>\<expected value>....

where \<prefix> is "" for `CHECK_EQUAL` and `CHECK_EQUAL_VARIANCE`.  "!=", ">", "<" for `CHECK_NOT_EQUAL`, `CHECK_GREATER` and `CHECK_LESS` respectively.

Well, you are probably right that I don't need to do this, however, this is called from `get_columns` which eventually uses the following query to get the `kwargs` to this function.

```sql
            SELECT
              n.nspname as "schema",
              c.relname as "table_name",
              att.attname as "name",
              format_encoding(att.attencodingtype::integer) as "encode",
              format_type(att.atttypid, att.atttypmod) as "type",
              att.attisdistkey as "distkey",
              att.attsortkeyord as "sortkey",
              att.attnotnull as "notnull",
              pg_catalog.col_description(att.attrelid, att.attnum)
                as "comment",
              adsrc,
              attnum,
              pg_catalog.format_type(att.atttypid, att.atttypmod),
              pg_catalog.pg_get_expr(ad.adbin, ad.adrelid) AS DEFAULT,
              n.oid as "schema_oid",
              c.oid as "table_oid"
            FROM pg_catalog.pg_class c
            LEFT JOIN pg_catalog.pg_namespace n
              ON n.oid = c.relnamespace
            JOIN pg_catalog.pg_attribute att
              ON att.attrelid = c.oid
            LEFT JOIN pg_catalog.pg_attrdef ad
              ON (att.attrelid, att.attnum) = (ad.adrelid, ad.adnum)
            WHERE n.nspname !~ '^pg_'
              AND att.attnum > 0
              AND NOT att.attisdropped
            UNION
            SELECT
              view_schema as "schema",
              view_name as "table_name",
              col_name as "name",
              null as "encode",
              col_type as "type",
              null as "distkey",
              0 as "sortkey",
              null as "notnull",
              null as "comment",
              null as "adsrc",
              null as "attnum",
              col_type as "format_type",
              null as "default",
              null as "schema_oid",
              null as "table_oid"
            FROM pg_get_late_binding_view_cols() cols(
              view_schema name,
              view_name name,
              col_name name,
              col_type varchar,
              col_num int)
            ORDER BY "schema", "table_name", "attnum";
```

    yield

```
TEMPORARY commit: implement test for #90

This commit implements a test for #90 (logging customisation) that is
wide-ranging as it (ab)uses all other tests in the suite to also check
that the feature implemented in the above-mentioned pull request works.

As the implications are currently not completely clear, this commit is
marked as temporary and will either be discarded or merged into a
previous commit in the pr before it is merged.

The most important open question is:
If a change breaks #90, will this be visible to the developers?
The tests in the suite run other tests using the `tempdir` facility and
this hacky implementation just adds a `conftest.py` to each of the
temporary directories. Will the message from the assert message ever
reach the output of the pytest-timeout test suite? Can that be somehow
enforced from within the child-tests or their pytest runner?
```

>> It's possible to be negative if the current process run takes longer than the previous one did.
> 
> When would that happen?

In exactly the case I've described, I think. You ran the process in the past: it took 1.5 seconds to run. You're running it again, and racing it against a cache lookup. > Seems like a big dependency to bring in for a [4 line function](https://github.com/pytoolz/toolz/blob/e9bc1e150b7b1ea1bec300a55382ed564b215f41/toolz/itertoolz.py#L548).

Cytoolz offers 2 benefits: clean code and speed.
The speed it brings is often sufficient to avoid needing to cythonize or port code to C leading to further code quality benefits as well
Depending on it brings in a moderately large number of small utility functions only a small number of which is likely to be used in any given PR. In each case it is easy to either reimplement an equivalent function or find a work around. In the long run though that means either having most of the library reimplemented possibly multiple times throughout our codebase and/or a lot of workarounds. Individual reimplementations are unlikely to get cythonized, so the codebase misses out on the performance benefit, in some cases leading to unnecessary C ports.

This could by demonstrated by refactoring a large piece of  code that is performance bottleneck. I will do more refactoring and optimization in this file for now to see how many more cytoolz uses turn up. So far i have been doing it method by method to keep the PRs small but a combine PR would demonstrate the cumulative effect

`OT_ERROR_INVALID_ARGS` is returned in two cases:
1. the multicast address is invalid (i.e. not starting with `ff`)
2. posix-nets adds a multicast address like ff02::2 to Thread stack, which is considered a `fixed` multicast address.

Case 1 is apparently wrong, but case 2 is fine. What really happens in practice is case 2, so ignoring `OT_ERROR_INVALID_ARGS` should be fine in most cases.

Maybe we can use another error code (e.g. `OT_ERROR_REJECTED` ) for case 2 so that it can also be ignored gracefully.

I see the advantage of using the handler as it is, but also for an up-counting filehandler; I'm not sure what the best approach is. A modification of the RotatingFileHandler doesn't seem to be very difficult though:

```
class forwardCountingRotatingFileHandler(RotatingFileHandler):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.baseFilenamebase = self.baseFilename
        self.file_number = 0

    def doRollover(self):
        """
        Do a rollover, as described in __init__().
        """
        if self.stream:
            self.stream.close()
            self.stream = None

        self.file_number += 1
        self.baseFilename = self.rotation_filename("%s.%d" % (self.baseFilenamebase, self.file_number))

        if not self.delay:
            self.stream = self._open()
```

An additional advantage is that in this case the BackupCount (while still a valid keyword argument) doesn't have any impact anymore.

I haven't gone too far in depth with the analysis but I'm worried about these lines and the shared responsibility over the `top` structure. 

First and most basically it seems like if this cleanup is going to happen at all it should be in a function in `shared/source/absfont/absfont.c`, probably called `abfFreeTopDict()` to match `abfInitTopDict()`. 

But that's a detail. The more pressing problem is that `top` is typically passed into `ufoBegFont()` as a double-pointer, which is then initialized and also stored in the `ufoCtx`. This is not ideal to begin with -- it would be preferable if `ufoBegFont()` could set the UFO `h->top` back to NULL before returning -- but if the ctx will still be used and needs it that may be impractical.

Let's assume that the double-pointer `top` is only valid a) while the ufoCtx is allocated and b) between calls to `ufoBegFont()` (It seems like one can call `ufoEndFont()` and not loose anything, as that's just for closing files and such?) 

In that case the ufoCtx top structure may be re-used (right? there's only one in the `txCtx` ...), and that's a problem given that `abfInitFontDict()` does't check for and free already-allocated values. A normal thing here would be to have `abfReInitTopDict()` which calls `abfFreeTopDict()` first and then `abfInitTopDict()`, and just use the `ReInit` version when you may be in a reuse situation. But the code could also just call `abfFreeTopDict()` first. 

Doing this safely is going to require a bit of analysis of the specific lifetime of the `top` structure.

All GraphQL compiler errors are subtypes of `GraphQLError`, but we never raise `GraphQLError` itself, instead opting for a subtype: any error the compiler raises passes `isinstance(e, GraphQLError)` but fails `type(e) == GraphQLError`.

I'm suggesting we could do the same for `SchemaTransformError`: have all exceptions raised from inside the `schema_transformation` module be derived from `SchemaTransformError` but always raise one of its subtypes instead of raising it directly. This is nice because then if you write `try ... except SchemaTransformError` you are explicitly stating that you intend to catch *all* such errors. Currently, since things can raise `SchemaTransformError` itself on its own, this is not true — there's no way to catch *just those errors* without also catching everything else, i.e. the intent behind that same `try ... except SchemaTransformError` would be unclear.

I have a few suggestions:
1. Change `_get_instructions` to `_get_answers`.
Internally we already have the concept of `_get_answer`, so this will be a nice wrapper around that function to orchestrate the algorithm of fetching the question links and then the answers.

1. I like to use JSON like the bread in a sandwich. As the code is now written, `_get_instructions` is returning JSON which must be `json.loads`'d and checked before it is returned to the user. There are several cluster metrics, I did not find h2o documentation for them, but some other [source](https://sites.google.com/site/dataminingatuoc/k-means) says:

**betweenss:** is the between clusters sum of squares. In fact it is the mean of distances between cluster centers. One expects, this ratio, to be as higher as possible, since we would like to have heterogenous clusters.

**withinss:** is the within cluster sum of squares. So it results in a vector with a number for each cluster. One expects, this ratio, to be as lower as possible for each cluster, since we would like to have homogeneity within the clusters.

**tot_withinss** = sum ( withinss )
**totss** =  tot_withinss + betweenss

As explained in https://github.com/ni/nimi-python/pull/1738#discussion_r905256369 and https://github.com/ni/nimi-python/pull/1738#discussion_r905261737, the calling code (i.e. the `AttributeEnumWithConverter class) has no context info about the supported values for conversion, so it cannot provide more detailed info after it catches the KeyError.

The data flows look like below:

**Getting attribute value:**
Driver runtime --> (convert to `Enum` with enum instantiation, which throws `ValueError`) --> Converter (which throws `KeyError`) --> User

**Setting attribute value:**
User --> Converter (which throws `KeyError`) --> (passes `enum,value` into C API) --> Driver runtime

If we want consistency across both converters, we can either:
1. Throw `ValueError` from both converters, then in the `AttributeEnumWithConverter` class:
    - Don't do anything for `__set__()` (let the `ValueError` with a list of valid values passes through to user)
    - Catch `ValueError` in `__get__()` and re-raise as `DriverTooNewError`
2. Throw `KeyError` from both converters, then in the `AttributeEnumWithConverter` class:
    - Catch `KeyError` in `__set__()` and re-raise as `ValueError` with generic error message
    - Catch `KeyError` and `ValueError` in `__get__()` and re-raise as `DriverTooNewError`

I'm going with option 2 (throwing `KeyError` from both converters now. Feel free to let me know if you prefer option 1.

Yeah, I had a fair amount of trouble deciding on an overall mental model here.  Consuming a `CoarsenedTarget` in `CompileJavaSourceRequest` was where I landed.  I also waffled for quite a while on spliting this into two rules: one for dealing with expanding and coarsening targets, and one lower level rule for actually running the compilation against a set of `JavaSources` fields.

I'm open to changing this, but let me explain my thought process:

> if you're compiling recursively, some nodes in the recursion not having sources shouldn't short-circuit the recursion.

The whole crux of the problem with `javac` is limiting my dependency classpath to only the current compilation unit's **direct** dependencies.  We don't want the full transitive closure on the classpath of the current compilation unit, by design, even if we might indirectly, recursively build it in order to build our direct dependencies.  So we're really distinguishing here between our compile-time (direct dep) classpath, and a "runtime" classpath that is likely the closure's classpath.  This is why the recursion eagerly terminates when it concludes that the current component doesn't produce any classpath entries: we don't need to build it, which means we don't need its deps (only used to build it), which means we don't recursively compile its deps.

But by direct dependency, we really mean direct **source** dependency, or possibly some other kind of resource dependency that can somehow contribute to the classpath but isn't yet modeled.  This is where things get sticky, because target expansion sort of changes the definition of "direct" dependency.

> You can see how the graph ends up being complete in the unit tests: the CoarsenedTargets for the base targets (dep, t1, and t2) depend on their own files.

That test only uses explicit file dependencies; it doesn't cover the case I'm covering here where, e.g. `t1` depends on `dep` instead of `dep.txt:dep`.

An example:

`A.java` -> `B` -- (expands to) --> (`B.java` and `B2.java`).

If we use target expansion, we'll automatically get `A.java` -> `B.java`;  `A.java` -> `C.java`; and `B.java` <--> `C.java`.  So subtarget expansion ends up getting us our natural notion of direct dependencies.  Just coarsening `B` will not do this; we need to do both.  See `javac_test.py:test_compile_with_transitive_multiple_sources` in this PR for a concrete example of this.

Stepping way back: my mental model here was to operate over the nodes of an induced **coarsened, expanded** graph.  Then each rule invocation maps 1:1 with a coarsened, expanded component, and its only job is to make sure that its recursive calls are also over coarsened, expanded components.  Expanding gets us the right notion of direct dependencies in the presence of subtarget expansion, while coarsening gets us the right notion of a single compilation unit in the presence of source-target cycles.

I'm pretty confident this needs a more generic model, since we're going to need to eventually worry about other rules that can contribute to the compilation classpath, which means we're probably going to need to request a union product from our dependencies.  That's where I was imagining two layers of rules, where `compile_java_source` is lower level and not expected to think hard about how to expand its dependencies.  But without another example of a rule that produces files for the compilation classpath, I worry that attempting to model that is premature.  So I opted for the "direct" route here just to get `javac` working and exercising the existing coarsening/expansion machinery.

Again, I'm happy to change this if you have an idea of how to remodel it, but pay particular attention to the example in `javac_test.py:test_compile_with_transitive_multiple_sources` to make sure that that case is covered (or why that case is somehow not actually a valid graph to test against).

To add a new input you have to do 2 things:
1. Create the new node and add it to the graph:
```
Node* input = new Node();
input->name = "my_new_input";
input->op = "null";
g->nodes.push_back(input);
g->inputs.push_back(input);
```
2. Create a new tensor to correspond to the new node:
```
MXTensor* arg_ = res.alloc_arg("my_new_input",{1},MXContext::CPU(0),kFloat32);
```
Notice that the node name and tensor name must match. 

> The new input has unknown and arbitrary tensor shape.
> Do we handle that somewhere ?
> 

In step 2 above you specify the shape when creating the tensor. In the example its `{1}`.

> Also is this added to both the
> a) subgraph_op and
> b) the subgraph attr of the subgraph_op ?
> 
> We probably do not want that in the latter.
> 

After creating the node in the graph and the corresponding tensor, you'll probably want to connect that node to your subgraph node to do anything interesting with it like:
```
//connect new input to node
input->outputs.push_back({n,(int)(n->inputs.size())});
//connect node to new input
n->inputs.push_back({input,0});
```
Where `n` is your subgraph node. 

Also, you'll need to set the attr on your subgraph op to make sure this input doesnt participate in shape/type inference like: `n->attrs[MX_STR_EXTRA_INPUTS] = std::to_string(1);`

I thought about it again and feel it is indeed not possible. A GUI application can have such `upgrader.exe` to upgrade the product itself, but this can't be done for a console application.

Consider this invocation chain: `python.exe`(1) -> `upgrader.exe` -> `python.exe`(2)

- To keep the second `python.exe` connected to the original terminal context, every executable on the invocation chain must be alive and wait for its downstream executable to exit. In order words, if `python.exe`(1) subprocesses `upgrader.exe` and exits, then after `upgrader.exe` upgrades `python.exe`, subprocesses `python.exe`(2) and exits, it is no longer possible to connect `python.exe`(2) back to the original terminal context - the output is out of order.
- If the first `python.exe` is alive, `upgrader.exe` cannot update it.

-> A contradiction!

Consider the following code:

```py
import subprocess

p = subprocess.Popen(['python.exe', '-c', 'import time; time.sleep(3); print("awaken")'])
print('Exiting')
```

Output:

```
PS D:\cli\testproj> python.exe main.py
Exiting
PS D:\cli\testproj> awaken
|
```

The first `python.exe` will exits and gives control back to the terminal. The second `python.exe` can print, yes, but it is out of order.

How to solve it? Wait for the subprocess to exit:

```py
import subprocess

p = subprocess.Popen(['python.exe', '-c', 'import time; time.sleep(3); print("awaken")'])
p.wait()
print('Exiting')
```

Output:

```
PS D:\cli\testproj> python.exe main.py
awaken
Exiting
PS D:\cli\testproj> |
```

I have rethought how we think of the sources for the Debian packages. I was a bit resistant to the idea of sources, but then it actually turned out to be great. 

Given this declaration:

```
debian_package(
    name='sample-debian-package',
    description='Useful tools installed as Debian package.',
    packages=[':files'],
    sources=['sample-debian-package/**/*'],
    symlinks={"myapp": "pex-target-name"},
    install_prefix="/opt/mycompany/tools",
    tags=["tooling"],
)
```

We can specify what directories we may need to be included. We need DEBIAN/control for the very least, but there can be multiple directories from various places - so why not let user pick what will be included into the package I thought? So instead of the `DebianControlFile` I think of `DebianSources`.

An example directory:

```
$ tree sample-debian-package      
sample-debian-package
├── DEBIAN
│   └── control
├── empty
└── opt
    └── company
        └── data.txt
```

Sources makes it possible for us to pick what we want to include.

hey James. I had left it as it is because right now it is addressing a problem that occurs only when there is a single cluster unit, which is rarely seen on production deployments, so the performance impact is not that significant. My concern is that we are introducing a change in the library that is used by every charm, but right now we are testing just a single charm with this updated code, we don't fully know the impact of the change on other charms or other corner cases we don't know about. What I suspect could be a negative impact of assigning the value to the attribute is that if there is a window of opportunity that happens in other charms or other corner cases we don't know about (such as maybe a relation-joined hook running concurrently with something else), with the original code and proposed fix, that problem could possibly correct itself as part of the hook execution, as the attribute would continue to be None and re-evaluate next time the code is reached (which it does on almost every reactive handle). If we assign the value, then it will not be re-evaluated anymore. On the other hand, if it causes a problem it will allow us to spot and fix them (hopefully not after it is running in production). Overall, IMO, the risk/benefit isn't worth it, but I'm ok if you prefer to have it. I have made the change and tested in my lab and it works fine, but only tested this specific scenario.

* `comma == std::string::npos`: no comma in string so append '.0`
* `comma == value.length() - 1`: Comma is last character so append 0

* `else`: trim zeros from end of string presentation of double.  So instead of 10.00000000000000 function will return 10.0

I'd vote for `Block = Union[pyarrow.Table, List[Any]]` being the lesser of two evils, since it actually gives the real types of the block while this generic dummy type doesn't have any real relation to the actual content types, and the type variable `T` wouldn't apply to the `pyarrow.Table` case anyway. E.g., although `BlockAccessor.iter_rows()` returns `Iterator[T]` on the base class, `ArrowBlockAccessor.iter_rows()` returns `Iterator[ArrowRow]` since `ArrowBlock` doesn't follow the `Block[T]` type scheme.

The output type for `BlockAccessor.iter_rows()` will probably be `Iterator[Any]`, but that will be more accurate than `Iterator[T]` anyway.

One subtle issue with this strategy is that it reverses tab order between RTL and LTR, which is generally not supposed to happen because tab order should be independent of text direction.

In the quiz page we work around this for the 'Next/Previous' buttons by dynamically changing `dir`:

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/f6318032221d72c9e9e54d4f41285b357299acff/kolibri/plugins/learn/assets/src/views/ExamPage/index.vue#L46

Another nice effect here is that the more important 'next' button gets focus _before_ the 'previous' button even though it visually appears "second".
